focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 4

Once he'd decided to risk showing someone one page, he spent three days choosing which page to bring.

The manuscript had fifty-seven sheets — he had counted them twice, turning each one by the edges with his thumbs clear of the notation because even now he still half expected the material to react if he handled it like an ordinary page. Most of the sheets were dense with notation that moved when he looked at it, relationships between symbols shifting like tidal patterns he could not predict. But a few pages were sparser. One near the middle had what looked like paired entries: a symbol cluster on the left, a simpler arrangement on the right. If any page was a key, this was it.

He folded it carefully — the material was not paper and did not crease easily, taking the fold with a faint resistance, like bending something that was used to being flat — and slid it into his Ancient Runes textbook between the chapter on Elder Futhark variations and the one he had not read yet.

Ancient Runes was the last class on Wednesdays. The classroom was on the third floor, a small room that still smelled like a school — ink and old binding glue and the specific warmth of reference books that had been absorbing body heat from generations of students leaning over them. Professor Babbling had not changed the room this year. The shelves were still lined with comparative notation charts and stone fragment replicas and the framed rubbing of the Rök runestone that Babbling touched sometimes when she passed it, an absent, habitual gesture, like checking a doorknob.

The class was smaller this year. Fourteen students instead of twenty-two. Three had not returned to Hogwarts. Five had dropped the elective because OWL preparation was difficult to prioritize when the school was what it...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...s cleared from his sight. If he followed the hum now and his knee gave out properly, he'd end up facedown on some hidden floor with Filch making rounds and dawn not far off. The card had said *Not to be removed.* That felt less like advice than an old rule waiting to find out whether it still had teeth.

He repeated the directions under his breath until they stopped slipping. Then he closed the drawer.

He needed to leave. His knees were bad and his back was bad and it was sometime between three and four in the morning and he had Potions at nine. He left the room and went back down the corridor, and the corridor was cooperative this time — it led to a staircase that went down, and the staircase connected where he needed it to, and within twenty minutes he was at the barrel entrance to the Hufflepuff common room, tapping the rhythm, and the barrel opened, and the common room was dark and warm and smelled of earth and whatever the kitchens were baking for morning, and he went to his dormitory and got into bed without undressing and lay on his stomach because his back wouldn't tolerate the mattress any other way.

His shoulders and arms pulsed. His knees felt like they'd been worked over with hammers. His palms stuck to the sheet where the half-moon cuts had dried tacky. His right knee was definitely bleeding. He could feel it, a warm stickiness inside his trouser leg that was different from the rest of the pain, more ordinary, more familiar. Like a skinned knee from falling off a bicycle, except he hadn't fallen off anything. He'd been kneeling.

In the dark, with his face turned into the pillow and his shoes still on, he mouthed the route until sleep took him: past the narrow place, then up, then through the room of empty shelves, then left where the stone changed color.
